transcript.transcript: Testing, testing, testing one two. This is an interview with Eric Johnson of Idaho Legal Aid at their office in Boise, Idaho on November 26, 1997. This interview is being conducted by myself, Dale N. Duncan Jr. I'll tell you the first thing, Just tell me how long you've been with Idaho Legal Aid and what division you work in. I started in May of 1993 and I'm the director of the Migrant Farmworker Law Unit. What does the Idaho Migrant Farm Unit do? It's a component of Idaho Legal Aid. We also have an Indian law unit, and we provide legal services to migrant farm workers, as well as seasonal farm workers. And we used to have migrant attorneys and outreach workers in Idaho Falls, Pocatello, Twin Falls, and Caldwell. We had a half-time attorney in Twin Falls, and quarter-time attorneys in Idaho Falls and Pocatello, and then outreach workers in the summer, as well as a full-time attorney in Caldwell, plus the minor union director, plus a full-time year-round outreach worker. But because of staff cutbacks, really all we have now is myself at Caldwell and two half-time people who are working on an EPA pesticide grant. On an EPA pesticide grant. When did you first get involved with the issue of workers' compensation? Right when I started in May of 1993, I started with it. In March of 1996, we got the bill passed. The spring of 95 was when we got to the hospital and lost. And then so the, that was after that we had the interim committee, which means that in the spring of 94 we were unable to get out of the House Committee. The House Committee stuck, which means in the spring of 93 we couldn't get the bill even printed. So that's where we were when I started. Mike Duggan, who worked for Legal Aid for about ten years and got laid off in 1994 when the new Congress cut our funds by 40%. He was working on the issue and There was a legislator named Wally Wright from the Coeur d'Alene area who introduced the bill. That would have been House State Affairs, I think. So, the bill was not printed, it was House State Affairs. What they decided to do was they would pass it off to a standing committee of the House State Affairs in the summer of 1993 to look at it some more, I guess. They put it on their agenda and then, with no notice to anybody, took it off the agenda. So they didn't even look at it that summer. So then it was the next winter, January or February, we got better organized and I think at that point Jesse Brain co-sponsored the bill with Wally Wright. We had two days of hearings before the House State Affairs Committee and they voted it down. But they sent it to an interim committee to study over the summer. And that's kind of when things really took off. How long had Legal Aid been involved with this prior to your arrival here? Back in, I believe, 1972, there was a guy at Legal Aid named Joe Ziker. He was the He was the Miami Game Director back then and he was a really outgoing guy. He was working with some farm workers in the wilder area and he helped them incorporate themselves as a non-profit organization, the Idaho Farm Workers Association. They had a press conference, and I still have some of those old press clippings, where they were forming with the main goal of getting the ag exemption to the workers' comp law removed. So there was certainly some activity around then and it didn't get anywhere. Was it pretty much idle from that point on up until the mid-90s or early 90s? Yeah, there was one other effort. Oh, and I'd have to go through my stuff to find out when it was. And what I'd really like to do is put this stuff in some kind of order and give it to the archives. I'd give it to you first. I don't know what your time frame is. But somewhere in my stuff I know that, oh, in the early 80s I believe it was, maybe 1980 or 1982, something like that, There was a representative who made a push for it. I know he had sent out some letters to the rest of the house. He made a substantial effort to try to get some movement on it. And the guy's name is eluding me right now, but I could find that. He, like I think I told you before, He's the head of the Idaho Conservation League or something like that right now. But I could find that. And then, of course, in, boy, it's funny how the dates start to elude you after a couple of years later. I believe in 1972, the Idaho workers' comp law was recodified. And Jack Barrett, Boise attorney, was representing the management side on those negotiations. And John Greenfield Sr. Was representing the labor side. I'm not sure they were exactly representing, I guess. They were the main advocates. And Janet Barrett worked on this at least since 1972 and he deserves a lot of credit for getting the bill passed eventually because he's a management side attorney, he had a lot of credibility and because he knew his stuff so well and because he worked on this for so long he had a lot of credibility amongst the legislators. But back then he and John Greenfield tried to get the worker comp exemption removed as part of the recodification process and they were unsuccessful. I just wanted to add to that. John Greenfield's son, I assume they're both John Greenfield, well certainly the younger guy is John Greenfield. He was a big help in getting this passed in 1996. He does labor side of He does labor side of Berkuskron. He's a good guy to talk to. He knows the old stuff better than I do. Like I said, his dad was involved. John was involved for many years. When I was talking to Ernesto, he was talking about, I guess, coming up in 95, 96, there were a series of meetings that, they weren't the committee meetings, but they were like a series of just late night meetings between Ernesto and I assume you went in on a few of them. Can you tell me a little bit about that? What was the purpose of these meetings? Would have been referring to the meetings that Dean Cameron had to try to grease the skids for the bill for the 1996 session. So that would have been around January, right It was right at the beginning of the legislative session and Dean Cameron was the chair of the Senate – I guess it's Human Resources, It's the equivalent of the House Human Resources Committee. Dean Cameron was the chair and what he did is he got together all the ag employers and also Central areas. I also got in touch with Ernesto and I and Jack Barrett. We had three meetings I think it was three meetings, and each one was an hour or two. And really the purpose was, in retrospect, the purpose was to kind of deflate the agricultural employer's opposition. And I think it worked really well. What he did was, the first meeting, he got in front of a big chalkboard or whatever and wrote down the concerns of the ag employers and the concerns of the farm worker advocates and we discussed whether they were valid concerns or not and the ones that were red herrings and nicks, which was really helpful because that was part of the strategy the ag employers had always used was to throw out everything they could think of that might slow the process down. And then we boiled it down to what the real concerns were and through that process we pretty much, I guess, completely deflated the ag employer's opposition. They really had their arms twisted to get real about the thing. Dean had all the governor's folks there. He had the financial Oh, he had the financial people, the state insurance fund director, he had the state insurance department, and some real good guys. I can't believe I forgot their names now, but I can go through my stuff and probably find it now if I look through my notes. The ag employers were a lot more willing to listen to them than they were to listen to us. So when they raised issues, like the big thing was cost. We dealt a lot with what the cost was going to be and the governor's people had real good numbers that the cost wasn't going to cost them very much, that the workers' premiums were going down and the farmers and ranchers were always exaggerating what the premiums were going to be. So this kind of brought some reality to the process. And they tried to put big exceptions into the law that would make it meaningless. One main one was that if you hired workers through a farm labor contractor, the farmer himself wouldn't have to have workers comp. We were able to fend that off because if they'd succeeded in doing that, then they'd just hire a farm labor contractor and then the farm labor contractor wouldn't provide the comp either and we'd be no better off than we had been before. The way the law is, the way the workers' comp law is in any other industry, if you hire a subcontractor, like say construction, and the subcontractor doesn't have the insurance, doesn't have the workers' comp insurance, then the general contractor is responsible and That guarantees that the general contractor will assure that either he's got the copper plates or the subcontractor does. So Dean Cameron, he was originally opposed to having workers' compensation cover these workers? That I guess we don't know officially because when we lost in March of 1995 on the House floor. We got a vote from the House members but we never got to the Senate. Each year we tried first in the House so we had no idea what the Senate would have done. In 1996 we went in the Senate first and the reason for that was, kind of like I explained before, once the interim committee did its work and came up with a compromise bill, the governor really started putting his weight behind it. They were just a lot more politically savvy and adept than we were. Their idea was to go through the Senate where they were pretty sure they could get it passed and built some momentum for the House. So they went through Dean Cameron's committee in the House. I think he said something like when the Governor first approached him he wasn't sure but when he learned all the facts he was in favor and he certainly did a real nice job for us in getting it done. How pivotal was the role of Governor Matt? It was absolutely critical. Without bad support it had zero chance. Without BAT pushing it hard it had little chance. In 1995 when we lost on the House floor, Governor Badd had already gone on record in his State of the State in December of 1994, January of 1995, whenever he gets the state of the state, just before the legislative session starts. He put in a whole paragraph in there about farm workers deserving equal treatment and deserving workers' comp. So it was clear that he was behind it, but at the time the focus for the governor was on property tax relief and he didn't make any noise about the workers' comp issue. So it got shot down on the House floor. But then in 1996 it was a whole different story because like I said the skids were greased in the Senate and as it turned out We thought we built up all this momentum. We got through the Senate committee 5-1 or 6-1, whatever it was. Then we got through the Senate on a landslide vote, 33-2 or something like that. Whatever it was, 30-5 or some really odd number, overwhelming number. We thought landslide vote, 33 to 2 or something like that. We thought we were in great shape. But then Representative Schaefer in the House of Human Resources bottled it up for as long as he could. That took a lot of steam out of it and we started getting worried he wouldn't bring it up. Finally someone pushed him to do it. There was that last-ditch effort by Representative Newcomb, the House Majority Leader, to derail the whole process by offering this kind of phony alternative. I would overcome that at the end. How much of a real threat was that? It was a big threat because Newcomb had a lot of authority. We were looking at a pretty close vote in the House and the governor had to do everything he could to round up those Republican votes. A lot of them were farmers or have farmer constituencies. It was very difficult for them to vote for this. What Newcomb was saying, and I shouldn't say it was phony because I think in his own mind he thought that it was legit. What he was trying to do was say farmers would either have to buy a workers' comp for their employees or provide an alternative that provided some coverage for medical bills, lost wages, and that was mainly it. He was saying that you could buy separate policies that would be cheaper than workers' comp and provide the same coverage. He came up with several variations of this And we dissected each one and each time it provided a lot less coverage than the workers' comp did and it was debatable whether we would have made it cheaper. And because it was kind of a last minute effort, we didn't really have time to really fine tune every last bit of it. So in the end that kind of gave way and we won by a pretty safe margin. We talked before about the possibility of a lawsuit coming about once things start getting quiet down. Can you tell us about the lawsuit? How close were you to filing it? We've been talking about the lawsuit since I started in 1993. It was always an option. Was always an option. I did research in 1993 and 1994 but not in a real serious vein I don't think. When we went to the Interim Committee in the summer of 1994, like I said before, it was a real pivotal appointments process. We had three hearings of this Interim Committee, three hearings or meetings, whatever you want to call them, plus a couple meetings in between where just the farm worker side met with the ag side. Eventually we kind of hammered out this compromise bill. And we're kind of lucky that when we got to the legislature in the spring of 1995 that the ag employers reneged on the deal because it wasn't the complete victory that we eventually got. For example, we had settled on an exemption for small farmers that would allow them to not have to buy workers comp if they had one or less employees plus they could have an additional 1, 500 in wages to another employee which is supposed to cover the so-called high school student comes in for a couple of weeks at harvest. We really fought and dickered over how big this exception was going to be because other states have exceptions ranging from five to ten workers and whether the work is mechanized or not. We were really trying to get a clean bill, so that's a big chunk of farm work that we left out. But we worked out this compromise and there were a lot of different parts to it. Some of the stuff was really kind of ludicrous, the stuff that the employers wanted in there. But then once we got to the House Human Resources in 1995, they reneged on it. And then we did get through the Human Resources, right? We did get through the House of Human Resources on a closed vote. I think it was 7-5, something like that. That was really tense because two of our votes weren't there while they were discussing it. I was walking around the legislative chambers trying to find the two people and I was certain that they didn't show up because they didn't want to stick their necks out. But there was some other reason and they got there and we got the votes. But we lost on the house floor. The ag employers bought it tooth and nail. That was really bitter because we negotiated in good faith with them and then they reneged on it. We thought the process was dead and at that point we decided to go the litigation route and I worked with Jack Barrett on putting a suit together and the hardest part was finding the plaintiffs. And over that summer, summer and fall I guess, of 95, I did find three pretty sympathetic cases of farm workers who'd been injured, farmers didn't have comp, and on the instance of one guy, he had a crappy trailer that the farmer provided, and when he got injured, not only did the employer not pay for his medical bills, not pay for his lost wages, he fired him and evicted him. So it presented a very sympathetic case. We had two other guys and we drafted the complaint and we were pretty much ready to go. We did all our research, we really worked out every little thing. We figured out our strategy, it was an equal protection argument. We were ready to go and like I said it took us a while and we were getting up to probably October of 1995 And we were about ready to file it and we've been talking to Michelle Cole of the Statesman and she was getting indications that we were going to have a better shot this year, the governor would be more supportive. At that point we decided we better hold off because we don't want to get people mad if we really have another shot at this. So we decided to hold off until the end of the session and if they got voted down again we would file at that point. Unfortunately, we did hold off the investment of Otero. You said there is equal protection. Can you explain that a little bit? The argument we were going to make under both the Idaho and United States constitutions because it comes out a little bit different because Idaho has, while its equal protection clause is pretty much identical to the one in the U.S. Constitution in the 14th Amendment. Idaho has interpreted it more broadly, slightly more broadly, than the United States Supreme Court. So we had an argument under both. And we figured our Idaho argument was a little bit stronger because there was a line of cases that had a fairly broad interpretation of the Equal Protection Clause. And kind of just to give you some idea of how it works, A law will violate the equal protection clause of the 14th Amendment of the United States Constitution if it creates either a suspect classification or violates a fundamental right. And so your classic suspect classification would be race. And we didn't really have that, although we were going to make an argument that because the vast majority of farm workers were Hispanic. It was kind of an indirect way of discriminating against Hispanics. An example of fundamental right would be the right to marriage, the right to vote, the right to interstate travel, those kinds of things. We didn't have that implicated. The right to insurance coverage when you're injured wasn't a fundamental right. So our argument under the U.S. Constitution was a little weak. But fortunately under the Idaho Constitution, they'd interpret it a little bit more broadly, And they had kind of a three-tiered scheme. The federal analysis is a two-tiered scheme. If you don't have a suspect classification or a fundamental right, then the test you use is a rational basis test. You just have to prove that the, well the state would just have to prove there's a rational basis for the classification and that's easy to do. A rational basis could be it saves farmers money and so it's good for the state of Idaho because we have lots of farmers. So you can hardly ever win there. And I should tell you that the classification in this case was agricultural employees versus all other workers in Idaho. So, but under the, in the 70s, the U.S. Supreme Court started kind of carving out this middle ground and it never really kind of got anywhere. And Idaho kind of went off in that direction too. But Idaho got a little bit farther. So we were going to try to go with this middle tier scrutiny. Off the top of my head I can't remember exactly what the exact language was, but it was something like you would have this middle level of scrutiny. You would scrutinize the classification of law. It was kind of middle tier scrutiny, if it was an unusually sensitive class or violated important rights. So kind of somewhere in between. And we thought Farm workers were an unusually sensitive class. We thought we could make a pretty good argument there.

story.title: Johnson_Erik_OH 2004 Side A
story.description: Recorded on November 26, 1997. Topic is Johnson's work as the director of the Migrant Farmworker Unit under Idaho Legal Aid.
